food_name: Nasi Uduk (Jakarta Coconut Rice with Anchovies and Omelette)
food_subgroup: Coconut milk-cooked rice with Indonesian accompaniments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Nasi Uduk. Closest parent is a coconut milk-cooked rice dish with accompaniments. No exact URI assigned.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice cooked in coconut milk with lemongrass and pandan, Indonesian nasi uduk style
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A00PB is the closest FoodEx2 parent for coconut milk-cooked rice; no exact Nasi Uduk code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Nasi Uduk or Indonesian coconut rice dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Nasi Uduk weighs approximately 400g, comprising roughly 200g coconut rice, 30g crispy anchovies, 60g fried egg, 60g tempe goreng, 30g potato fritter, and 20g sambal.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table — Nasi Uduk; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data for coconut milk-cooked rice, fried anchovies, and egg
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table; USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: FAO/INFOODS SEA Table provides the primary composite values for Nasi Uduk as a dish. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for coconut milk-cooked rice (NDB 20444), dried anchovies (NDB 15001), and fried egg (NDB 01128) were used to cross-validate and fill micronutrient gaps. Values represent a weighted composite of the full plate (rice ~50%, anchovies ~7.5%, egg ~15%, tempe ~15%, potato fritter ~7.5%, sambal ~5%).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Cooked in Coconut Milk — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate based on coconut milk-cooked rice entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Nasi Uduk in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 68 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: rice cooked in coconut milk, which has a slightly lower GI than plain steamed white rice (GI ~72) due to the fat content of coconut milk slowing gastric emptying. Glycemic load per 400g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (68 × 90) / 100 = 61.2, rounded to 60.8 per input source. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from crispy fried anchovies (ikan teri), sambal, and salted coconut rice
hypertension.guidance: A standard 400g plate delivers approximately 1,420mg sodium — nearly 60% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000mg — driven primarily by the crispy anchovies; individuals with hypertension should request reduced anchovy portions or choose a lower-sodium sambal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~60.8) from a large rice portion (~200g cooked) with medium GI of 68
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 400g plate delivers approximately 90g available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~60.8, which is very high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a plate (~200g) and pair with additional protein and fibre to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (5.5g/100g) from full-fat coconut milk and deep-frying, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from coconut milk (predominantly medium-chain triglycerides) and the high sodium from anchovies and sambal represent dual cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings and request steamed or reduced-coconut-milk rice where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Uduk is Jakarta's iconic Betawi coconut rice dish — the Indonesian counterpart to Singapore's Nasi Lemak. White jasmine rice is cooked by the absorption method in full-fat coconut milk infused with lemongrass, salam leaf (Indonesian bay leaf), and pandan leaf, producing fragrant, slightly rich, coconut-flavoured rice with a soft, slightly sticky texture. It is traditionally served as a breakfast meal with a combination of accompaniments: crispy deep-fried dried anchovies (ikan teri), a fried or scrambled egg, tempe goreng (fried fermented soybean cake), a fried potato fritter, and sambal (chilli paste). The dish is calorie-dense at approximately 195 kcal per 100g, with a notable saturated fat contribution (5.5g/100g) from the coconut milk and a high sodium load (355mg/100g) driven by the anchovies and sambal. A full 400g plate delivers approximately 780 kcal, 90g carbohydrate (GL ~60.8), and 1,420mg sodium. Calcium from whole-bone anchovies (55mg/100g) is nutritionally notable. Tempeh provides anti-inflammatory isoflavones and plant protein. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is widely available at Indonesian food courts, Betawi restaurants, and Indonesian hawker stalls in Singapore.

confidence_reason: FAO/INFOODS SEA Table provides a primary composite entry for Nasi Uduk as a dish, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component data. No single approved source provides an exact per-100g breakdown for this specific Jakarta-style preparation with all accompaniments; values represent a weighted composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the Nasi Uduk composite dish (including coconut milk, anchovies, egg, and tempe) are not separately reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Table or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Table. All other per_100g values are numeric and sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA Table cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No core macro is zero; all macros are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a coconut milk-cooked rice dish with protein-rich accompaniments. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on coconut milk-cooked rice in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load of 60.8 per 400g serving is very high and clinically significant for diabetic management. Sodium of 355mg/100g (1,420mg per 400g plate) is primarily attributable to crispy fried anchovies and sambal. FoodEx2 code A00PB is the closest parent code for coconut milk-cooked rice; no exact Nasi Uduk code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
